ZZeZePeDj[
d(d)
d)eFeP
Z\eZe@eDj]
d*d+
d+eFe@
Define the SeriesGroupBy and DataFrameGroupBy
classes that hold the groupby interfaces (and some implementations).
These are user facing as the result of the ``df.groupby(...)`` operations,
which here returns a DataFrameGroupBy object.
annotations)
namedtuple)
partial)
dedent)

	ArrayLike

    Create a property for a GroupBy subclass to dispatch to DataFrame/Series.
    Parameters

A/usr/lib64/python3.9/site-packages/pandas/core/groupby/generic.py

property)
parent_methodrA
generate_propertyf
frozenset[str])
	allowlistc
    Create GroupBy member defs for DataFrame/Series names in a allowlist.
    Parameters
    ----------
    klass : DataFrame or Series class
        class where members are defined.
    allowlist : frozenset[str]
        Set of names of klass methods to be constructed
    Returns
    -------
    class decorator
    Notes
    -----
    Since we don't want to override methods explicitly defined in the
    base class, any such name is skipped.
    c

dBdC
Z!dSd

dGdH
Z#dTdKdL
SeriesGroupBy
Iterable[Series]
returnc
_selected_objr>
_iterate_slices
SeriesGroupBy._iterate_slicesa
    Examples
    --------
    >>> s = pd.Series([1, 2, 3, 4])
    >>> s
    0    1
    1    2
    2    3
    3    4
    dtype: int64
    >>> s.groupby([1, 1, 2, 2]).min()
    1    1
    2    3
    dtype: int64
    >>> s.groupby([1, 1, 2, 2]).agg('min')
    1    1
    2    3
    dtype: int64
    >>> s.groupby([1, 1, 2, 2]).agg(['min', 'max'])
       min  max
    1    1    2
    2    3    4
    The output column names can be controlled by passing
    the desired column names and aggregations as keyword arguments.
    >>> s.groupby([1, 1, 2, 2]).agg(
    ...     minimum='min',
    ...     maximum='max',
    ... )
       minimum  maximum
    1        1        2
    2        3        4
    .. versionchanged:: 1.3.0
        The resulting dtype will reflect the return value of the aggregating function.
    >>> s.groupby([1, 1, 2, 2]).agg(lambda x: x.astype(float).min())
    1    1.0
    2    3.0
    dtype: float64
templateZ
seriesZ

input
examplesc

z!SeriesGroupBy._cython_agg_general
+Mapping[base.OutputKey, Series | ArrayLike]
        Wraps the output of a SeriesGroupBy aggregation into the expected result.
        Parameters
        ----------
        output : Mapping[base.OutputKey, Union[Series, ArrayLike]]
            Data to wrap.
        Returns
        -------
        Series
        Notes
        -----
        In the vast majority of cases output will only contain one element.
        The exception is operations that expand dimensions, like ohlc.
        rg
lenrj
iterr
_wrap_aggregated_outputg
z%SeriesGroupBy._wrap_aggregated_outputc
        Wraps the output of a SeriesGroupBy aggregation into the expected result.
        Parameters
        ----------
        output : dict[base.OutputKey, Union[Series, np.ndarray, ExtensionArray]]
            Dict with a sole key of 0 and a value of the result values.
        Returns
        -------
        Series
        Notes
        -----
        output should always contain one element. It is specified as a dict
        for consistency with DataFrame methods and _wrap_aggregated_output.
        rg
_wrap_transformed_output
z&SeriesGroupBy._wrap_transformed_outputFr1
list[Any] | Noner
not_indexed_samerT
        Wrap the output of SeriesGroupBy.apply into the expected result.
        Parameters
        ----------
        data : Series
            Input data for groupby operation.
        keys : Index
            Keys of groups that Series was grouped by.
        values : Optional[List[Any]]
            Applied output for each group.
        not_indexed_same : bool, default False
            Whether the applied outputs are not indexed the same as the group axes.
        Returns
        -------
        DataFrame or Series
        r

_wrap_transform_fast_result-
z)SeriesGroupBy._wrap_transform_fast_resultr
        Return a copy of a Series excluding elements from groups that
        do not satisfy the boolean criterion specified by func.
        Parameters
        ----------
        func : function
            To apply to each group. Should return True or False.
        dropna : Drop groups that do not pass the filter. True by default;
            if False, groups that evaluate False are filled with NaNs.
        Notes
        -----
        Functions that mutate the passed object can produce unexpected
        behavior or errors and are not supported. See :ref:`gotchas.udf-mutation`
        for more details.
        Examples
        --------
        >>> df = pd.DataFrame({'A' : ['foo', 'bar', 'foo', 'bar',
        ...                           'foo', 'bar'],
        ...                    'B' : [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],
        ...                    'C' : [2.0, 5., 8., 1., 2., 9.]})
        >>> grouped = df.groupby('A')
        >>> df.groupby('A').B.filter(lambda x: x.mean() > 3.)
        1    2
        3    4
        5    6
        Name: B, dtype: int64
        Returns
        -------
        filtered : Series
        c
<lambda>[

__classcell__rA

d5d6
dbd7d8
d9d

d=d>
d?d	
d@dA
dBd,
dCdD
Z!dEd
dGdH
Z"dEd
dIdJ
Z#d?d
dLdM
Z$dNdO
dPdQ
Z%dNd
dRdS
dTdU
Z'ddd
dWdX
e)j*j+
dZd[
e)j,j+
d\d]
Z,e-Z.
DataFrameGroupBya
    Examples
    --------
    >>> df = pd.DataFrame(
    ...     {
    ...         "A": [1, 1, 2, 2],
    ...         "B": [1, 2, 3, 4],
    ...         "C": [0.362838, 0.227877, 1.267767, -0.562860],
    ...     }
    ... )
    >>> df
       A  B         C
    0  1  1  0.362838
    1  1  2  0.227877
    2  2  3  1.267767
    3  2  4 -0.562860
    The aggregation is for each column.
    >>> df.groupby('A').agg('min')
       B         C
    A
    1  1  0.227877
    2  3 -0.562860
    Multiple aggregations
    >>> df.groupby('A').agg(['min', 'max'])
        B             C
      min max       min       max
    A
    1   1   2  0.227877  0.362838
    2   3   4 -0.562860  1.267767
    Select a column for aggregation
    >>> df.groupby('A').B.agg(['min', 'max'])
       min  max
    A
    1    1    2
    2    3    4
    Different aggregations per column
    >>> df.groupby('A').agg({'B': ['min', 'max'], 'C': 'sum'})
        B             C
      min max       sum
    A
    1   1   2  0.590715
    2   3   4  0.704907
    To control the output names with different aggregations per column,
    pandas supports "named aggregation"
    >>> df.groupby("A").agg(
    ...     b_min=pd.NamedAgg(column="B", aggfunc="min"),
    ...     c_sum=pd.NamedAgg(column="C", aggfunc="sum"))
       b_min     c_sum
    A
    1      1  0.590715
    2      3  0.704907
    - The keywords are the *output* column names
    - The values are tuples whose first element is the column to select
      and the second element is the aggregation to apply to that column.
      Pandas provides the ``pandas.NamedAgg`` namedtuple with the fields
      ``['column', 'aggfunc']`` to make it clearer what the arguments are.
      As usual, the aggregation can be a callable or a string alias.
    See :ref:`groupby.aggregate.named` for more.
    .. versionchanged:: 1.3.0
        The resulting dtype will reflect the return value of the aggregating function.
    >>> df.groupby("A")[["B"]].agg(lambda x: x.astype(float).min())
          B
    A
    1   1.0
    2   3.0r)
j#d	d
no results)
levelr
datetime)$r5
ilocrp

ignore_failures
Dropping invalid columns in r
Q is deprecated. In a future version, a TypeError will be raised. Before calling .z=, select only columns which should be valid for the function.

z(DataFrameGroupBy._can_use_transform_fastr
        Fast transform path for aggregations
        Fr
z,DataFrameGroupBy._wrap_transform_fast_resultc
z0DataFrameGroupBy._define_paths.<locals>.<lambda>c
BDataFrameGroupBy._define_paths.<locals>.<lambda>.<locals>.<lambda>r{
DataFrameGroupBy._define_paths)
	Exceptionrl
res_fastrA
DataFrameGroupBy._choose_path)
.transform is deprecated. In a future version, a TypeError will be raised. Before calling .transform, select only columns which should be valid for the transforming function.
z)Transform function invalid for data typesr
indsrg
subset
sgbrn
z(DataFrameGroupBy._transform_item_by_itemc
        Return a copy of a DataFrame excluding filtered elements.
        Elements from groups are filtered if they do not satisfy the
        boolean criterion specified by func.
        Parameters
        ----------
        func : function
            Function to apply to each subframe. Should return True or False.
        dropna : Drop groups that do not pass the filter. True by default;
            If False, groups that evaluate False are filled with NaNs.
        Returns
        -------
        filtered : DataFrame
        Notes
        -----
        Each subframe is endowed the attribute 'name' in case you need to know
        which group you are working on.
        Functions that mutate the passed object can produce unexpected
        behavior or errors and are not supported. See :ref:`gotchas.udf-mutation`
        for more details.
        Examples
        --------
        >>> df = pd.DataFrame({'A' : ['foo', 'bar', 'foo', 'bar',
        ...                           'foo', 'bar'],
        ...                    'B' : [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],
        ...                    'C' : [2.0, 5., 8., 1., 2., 9.]})
        >>> grouped = df.groupby('A')
        >>> grouped.filter(lambda x: x['B'].mean() > 3.)
             A  B    C
        1  bar  2  5.0
        3  bar  4  1.0
        5  bar  6  9.0
        r{
filter function returned a z
, but expected a scalar bool)
AttributeErrorr
DataFrameGroupBy.filterz DataFrameGroupBy | SeriesGroupByc
z'Cannot subset columns when using axis=1zmIndexing with multiple keys (implicitly converted to a tuple of keys) will be deprecated, use a list instead.
__getitem__)
DataFrameGroupBy.__getitem__)
        sub-classes to define
        return a sliced object
        Parameters
        ----------
        key : string / list of selections
        ndim : {1, 2}
            requested ndim of result
        subset : object, default None
            subset to act on
        r
group_keysrr
mutatedr
invalid ndim for _gotitem)
_gotitem
DataFrameGroupBy._gotitemr
z'DataFrameGroupBy._get_data_to_aggregate

zCDataFrameGroupBy._insert_inaxis_grouper_inplace.<locals>.<listcomp>r

        Wraps the output of DataFrameGroupBy aggregations into the expected result.
        Parameters
        ----------
        output : Mapping[base.OutputKey, Union[Series, np.ndarray]]
           Data to wrap.
        Returns
        -------
        DataFrame
        c
z<DataFrameGroupBy._wrap_aggregated_output.<locals>.<dictcomp>c

z(DataFrameGroupBy._wrap_aggregated_outputc
        Wraps the output of DataFrameGroupBy transformations into the expected result.
        Parameters
        ----------
        output : Mapping[base.OutputKey, Union[Series, np.ndarray, ExtensionArray]]
            Data to wrap.
        Returns
        -------
        DataFrame

z=DataFrameGroupBy._wrap_transformed_output.<locals>.<dictcomp>rg

z>DataFrameGroupBy._apply_to_column_groupbys.<locals>.<listcomp>r|
_apply_to_column_groupbys
z*DataFrameGroupBy._apply_to_column_groupbysc
        Compute count of group, excluding missing values.
        Returns
        -------
        DataFrame
            Count of values within each group.
        r
labelsZ
max_binr
count_level_2d)
maskedZ
counted
hfunc
z%DataFrameGroupBy.count.<locals>.hfuncr
temp_setattrr_
DataFrameGroupBy.countr
        Return DataFrame with counts of unique elements in each position.
        Parameters
        ----------
        dropna : bool, default True
            Don't include NaN in the counts.
        Returns
        -------
        nunique: DataFrame
        Examples
        --------
        >>> df = pd.DataFrame({'id': ['spam', 'egg', 'egg', 'spam',
        ...                           'ham', 'ham'],
        ...                    'value1': [1, 5, 5, 2, 5, 5],
        ...                    'value2': list('abbaxy')})
        >>> df
             id  value1 value2
        0  spam       1      a
        1   egg       5      b
        2   egg       5      b
        3  spam       2      a
        4   ham       5      x
        5   ham       5      y
        >>> df.groupby('id').nunique()
              value1  value2
        id
        egg        1       1
        ham        1       2
        spam       2       1
        Check for rows with the same id but conflicting values:
        >>> df.groupby('id').filter(lambda g: (g.nunique() > 1).any())
             id  value1 value2
        0  spam       1      a
        3  spam       2      a
        4   ham       5      x
        5   ham       5      y
        r
z*DataFrameGroupBy.nunique.<locals>.<lambda>c
DataFrameGroupBy.nunique)
skipnac
argmax
z9DataFrameGroupBy.idxmax.<locals>.func.<locals>.<listcomp>r
_reducer"
Z	nanargmaxr
z%DataFrameGroupBy.idxmax.<locals>.func
_get_axis_numberZ
_python_apply_generalrH

DataFrameGroupBy.idxmaxc

Z	nanargminr
z%DataFrameGroupBy.idxmin.<locals>.funcr

T)0rG

__future__r

textwrapr
typingr
numpyr
pandas._libsr
pandas._typingr
pandas.util._decoratorsr
pandas.core.dtypes.commonr
pandas.core.dtypes.missingr
pandas.corer!
